title: Stop Prompting Claude. Use Karpathy's Method Instead.
summary: Austin Marchese discusses Andrej Karpathy's method for using Claude and Claude Code to accelerate development. The approach is organized into three layers and positions itself as more effective than standard prompting techniques. Marchese frames this as enabling 10x faster building in 2026.
writeup: Distilling a framework from Andrej Karpathy's AISN 2026 talk, this video argues that effective AI use rests on three stacked layers: the spec (delivering human context to the model), the verifier (closing the feedback loop), and the environment (a persistent workspace that compounds over time). The through-line is that AI lacks intrinsic motivation and context, so every gap must be explicitly engineered rather than prompted away. Karpathy's diagnosis, illustrated with the car-wash walking fallacy that Claude, Gemini, Grok, and ChatGPT all failed, frames the entire method: the only lever that actually works is structural, not emotional.
key_points: Karpathy argues Claude's plan mode is too high-level and that the real move is working with the agent to design a detailed spec, starting by having Claude interview the user to surface the actual goal rather than just the stated task.

Effective spec creation follows three steps: uncover the true goal (not the task), work agile with tight-scoped checkpoints rather than handing over a waterfall of requirements, and be explicit enough that the model makes zero assumptions.

The verifier layer operates on Karpathy's animals-versus-ghosts distinction: LLMs have no intrinsic motivation and cannot self-correct from emotional signals, so the only reliable quality lever is a structured feedback loop, which Boris Cherney (creator of Claude Code) quantified as a 2 to 3x improvement in final output quality.

Running a second model as critic, using the Codex plugin inside a Claude Code session to cross-check complex builds, implements a cross-library sanity check that catches errors the first model cannot catch from its own training distribution.

Pulling external signal into verification, such as connecting Claude to the actual deployment environment to get a ground-truth pass/fail or feeding in historical report formats as reference, converts subjective quality checks into definitive oracles.

The environment layer centers on a persistent CLAUDE.md file injected automatically on every prompt, encoding standing rules like requiring a verification plan before any multi-step build, so correct behavior is structural rather than something that must be re-requested each session.
